title: One hot key melts a stage — salting fixes it

steps.narration: Same job, two strategies. We attach seller-level fraud stats (rolled up from fraud_scores) to every order — a sort-merge join on seller_id, 8 shuffle partitions. At production scale that is 240M orders, and seller S-777 owns 80M of them — one third of the table, exactly the 80/240 skew in our sample. Left: raw key. Right: the same key salted with a random 0–7 suffix.

after.note: join key: concat(seller_id, '_', floor(rand()*8)) — small side exploded once per salt

steps.narration: Shuffle write. Rows are routed by hash(key) % 8 — and hash('S-777') is a single value, so all 80M hot rows land in partition p3. Salting turns one key into eight (S-777_0 … S-777_7), ~10M rows each, and the strip levels out at ~30M per partition.

Reduce tasks launch, one per partition, two per executor. Six seconds in: seven of the left tasks are cruising through ~23M rows each, but p3 must sort-merge 80M alone — 8% done. On the right, every task carries ~30M and they move in lockstep.

Why is p3 falling behind faster than 3.5× the rows would predict? Its 9.6 GB sort does not fit the task's 2 GB of execution memory, so it spills — external-sort passes to disk. Skew is not linear pain: 3.5× the data becomes ~7× the time once you spill.

16 seconds: the salted stage finished at 7s — its slowest task read 3.7 GB, comfortably in memory. On the left, the seven normal tasks are done too; the stage cannot close until p3 does. This is the Spark UI pattern you will learn to smell: a stage parked at 7/8 tasks.

after.note: stage complete at 7s — all 8 tasks within 0.4s of each other

steps.narration: The bill for skew is not just time — it is paid-for cores doing nothing. Eight cores rented, one working: 87% of this stage's core-seconds are idle wait. The scheduler cannot help; a task is the unit of parallelism, and p3 is indivisible.

after.note: cluster released at 7s — downstream stages already running

steps.narration: 34 seconds. p3 is on its final merge pass — the bar sits at 96% while the wall clock keeps charging you. In a real UI this is the flat line at 199/200 tasks succeeded that pages you at 3am.
Done: 41s vs 7s — 5.9× from a single key. Same data, same cluster, same answer. The only change was making the hash function's life easier.

How salting stays correct: both sides must speak the same key language. The big side gets a random salt; the small side is exploded once per salt value — every seller row copied 8 times, so S-777_3 still finds its match. If you only salt one side, the join silently drops rows.

after.note:
orders.key = seller_id + '_' + rand(0..7)
seller_stats × explode(0..7) → seller_id + '_' + salt

steps.narration: The tradeoff, stated plainly: the small side grew 8× (40M seller rows → 320M shuffled), and any downstream aggregate needs a second pass to merge salted partials back to the real key. That tax is trivial next to a 34s straggler — but only for keys that are actually hot. Salt surgically, measure first, and check whether Spark 3 AQE skew-join handles it before you reach for the manual override.

before.note: no extra rows — and a 41s stage

after.note: 8× exploded small side + a merge pass — the price of a 5.9× faster stage
